title: Perfection
author: Vincenzo Latronico
comments: I guess since I just wrote my comments for 'no longer human' there are some parallels to be drawn between that book and this one? I suppose with the main characters in both being 'creatives' and with the sense of dread imposed in the novel. Both end with a similar sense of dissatisfaction. They are very different of course, the dread imposed by the horrors of contemporary life, which 'Perfection' is trying to hit home, is very different from the dread that comes with an inherent feeling of not belonging. They both left me with similar emotions, though another big difference between the two is the lack of conflict with the modern age in 'No Longer Human', at least to the extent of 'Perfection'. Focusing just on 'Perfection' I am not fully sure how to feel about the fight it picks with modern life. The book ends in 2019, with the couple leading a new life they deal with, not fully happy, though not restless. They have given up on chasing the joys of the ideal lives they led in their 20s. Even if you aren't thinking about how their world will be shaken up by the impeding global pandemic of the 2020s, I don't think you can imagine a happy ending for them. The author makes the lives they had led, one led surrounded by technology, and interfacing with it everyday, impossible to be fulfilling. I kind of like the dread here, the book brings into discussion other possible paths for them, a life as a vet, or a farmer, a job away from a laptop and css classes and minimalist web design, yet, you deal with the fact these other lives are not reality, and the anxiety that they might've been unhappy in this alternate life too. You are asked to assign a blame, if it is their faults, or the faults the contemporary age. Or a bit of both. I guess I find the main ideas in this book so interesting because of my own dissatisfaction with my life, partly imposed by vague ideas of futility imposed by living in the present. Though I want to live thinking I can seek happiness, whether that be naive or not. I'll deal with the downfall in my 40s.

title: No Longer Human
author: Osamu Dazai
comments: I didn't realize that this book was created in 1948, which, in retrospect (it's so easy to notice things in retrospect) is very obvious. But theres a modern sentiment in the novel that definitely makes part of it feel like it was created today.

title: Bury Our Bones in the Midnight Soil
author: V.E. Schwab
comments: It was not what I usually go for, and really didn't need to be 500 pages long, and even though it kind of droned on and has parts that were almost made to roll your eyes to, I still enjoyed reading it. Like vampires. Whatever I was with it

title: LOVE BULLET #1
author: INEE
comments: WHY DOES THE SECOND VOLUME COME TO THE STATES IN OCTOBER ??????? I can read it online... but like.. the only reason I bought a physical copy of this one is because every other manga has like a million volumes that I would need to buy, I wouldn't feel good if I didn't have the entire series, but like that's soooo expensive, and this series only has one volume! But now I have a commitment of waiting until october. IDK i set an alarm for myself I read the first volume kind of really quick so I guess it won't be that bad to catch up when october comes.. i hope they sell a copy at my local barnes & noble... Also I cried to volume 1 I wouldnt care this much if I didn't like it

title: The 7 1/2 Deaths of Evelyn Hardcastle
author: Stuart Turton
comments: Kind of the most fun I've had reading a book in a while!! Maybe I'll start reading more mysteries. I think I'm a sucker for seeing a bunch of clues come together, I realllly liked that part of Disco Elysium when I played that a little bit ago. The 7 1/2 Deaths of Evelyn Hardcastle don't disappoint in this aspect, there are sooo many moving parts.

title: The Design of Everyday Things
author: Donald Norman
comments: I think this changed my life in a very small way, this book will be at the front of my mind whenever I interact with any new product, and definitely whenever I run into a terribly made product.

title: On Earth We're Briefly Gorgeous
author: Ocean Vuong
comments: Striking and honest. I really enjoy when a book offers insight into the lives of others, through the common medium of struggle.

title: How Countries Go Broke
author: Ray Dalio
comments: Lots of incredibly valuable information is in here, giving a very understandable view of global cycles. Unfortunately it's not perfect and Ray Dalio suffers from sounding too much like a 76 year old founder of the largest hedge fund on planet Earth, at least in some sections of the book.

title: Azumanga Daioh Omnibus

comments: A poem is nothing like a piece of dust. scarily good literature I wished I had read earlier, but maybe I wouldn't have been fit to read it earlier.

comments: i learned

title: Y2K: How the 2000s Became Everything (essays on the future that never was)
author: Colette Shade
comments: I expected more and also less if that makes sense? I feel I didn't get exactly what I wanted with this book, I feel it didn't go far enough with its points and it's purpose in trying to emphasize how truly important the 2000s were. It's kind of scattered and Colette shade really wants to talk about politics (which isn't an issue but after reading idk if I want to hear colette shade talk about politics anymore). The book still had good information and was fun to read.

title: The Metamorphosis & Other Stories
author: Franz Kafka
comments: I have no doubt in my mind that The Metamorphosis itself is his magnum opus, but it was really interesting reading the other stories in this compilation.. I feel there's a 50% chance I would get along with Kafka, from his stories, I feel he would be a wildcard. Maybe 30%. He would be interesting to talk to.

title: Symposium or Drinking Party
author: Plato
comments: I completely forgot I had read this book and own this book and I stumbed upon it and everything came rushing back. I have little post-it notes peeking out of this one. I don't think I've read anything similar to this, and I really want to revisit now to recall some of the themes and talking points.

title: Seek You: A Journey Through American Loneliness
author: Kristen Radtke
comments: My college gave this one to me, and I really did enjoy it! nonfiction graphic novel about loneliness, which at the time I wasn't aware that I would be reading another nonfiction graphic novel this year. (Fun Home) I hope they give us another free book next year, and while I doubt it, in this case it doesn't hurt to hope
